prompt: Which choice best states the central idea of the passage?

choices.text: Giant kelp grows so slowly that it can take many years to reach the surface.
choices.rationale: The passage says kelp grows astonishingly fast, not slowly.

choices.text: Kelp forests are rich but fragile habitats that depend on a balance of species.
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage presents kelp forests as rich habitats whose health rests on species balance.

choices.text: Kelp forests are found mainly in warm, sandy waters near tropical beaches.
choices.rationale: The passage places these forests off cool, rocky coasts.

choices.text: Sea otters are harmful pests that destroy the kelp forests they live in.
choices.rationale: Otters protect the kelp; their loss lets urchins destroy it.

explanation: The passage frames kelp forests as productive yet fragile, balanced ecosystems.

stimulus.title: Forests Beneath the Waves
stimulus.text: Off some cool, rocky coasts grow forests that no hiker will ever walk through. Giant kelp, anchored to the seafloor, can stretch dozens of meters toward the surface, swaying in the current like trees in wind. These kelp forests shelter fish, snails, and otters, much as a woodland shelters birds and deer. The kelp grows astonishingly fast, sometimes half a meter in a single day, drinking sunlight near the surface. But the forest is fragile. Where otters vanish, sea urchins multiply and graze the kelp down to bare rock, leaving a barren the divers call an urchin desert. A single missing animal can unmake a whole forest.

prompt: Which choice best states the central idea of the passage?

choices.text: Folk weather signs mixed superstition with genuine insight that modern forecasting absorbed.
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage says folk signs blended superstition and real wisdom, the best of which forecasting absorbed.

choices.text: Modern forecasting completely discarded every part of older weather watching.
choices.rationale: The passage says modern forecasting absorbed, not discarded, the best parts.

choices.text: Folk weather knowledge was entirely accurate and never mistaken in any case.
choices.rationale: The passage says much folk knowledge was unreliable.

choices.text: Farmers ignored the sky and the land when trying to predict the weather.
choices.rationale: The passage shows farmers read the sky and land closely.

explanation: The passage balances the flaws and genuine insight of folk weather signs.

stimulus.title: Reading the Weather Before Radio
stimulus.text: Long before satellites mapped storms from orbit, farmers learned to read the sky and the land for signs of coming weather. A ring of haze around the moon, the smell of soil before rain, the behavior of swallows flying low: such clues were gathered into almanacs and passed down through families. Much of this folk knowledge was unreliable, mixing keen observation with superstition. Yet some of it held real wisdom, since the conditions that produce a hazy moon truly can precede rain. Modern forecasting has not so much replaced this older watching as absorbed its best parts, trading the rooster's crow for the radar's sweep.

prompt: Which choice best states the central idea of the passage?

choices.text: Plants are able to uproot themselves and walk to find better ground.
choices.rationale: The passage opens by noting a plant cannot walk.

choices.text: Seeds always grow best directly beneath the shade of the parent plant.
choices.rationale: The passage says seeds need to leave the parent's shadow.

choices.text: Wind is the only means by which any plant can spread its seeds.
choices.rationale: The passage describes wind, hooks, and fruit, not wind alone.

choices.text: Plants have evolved varied methods to disperse their seeds away from the parent.
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage surveys several strategies plants use to move their seeds.

explanation: The passage's focus is the variety of seed-dispersal strategies.

stimulus.title: Travelers Without Legs
stimulus.text: A plant cannot walk, yet its seeds must somehow leave the shadow of the parent and find open ground. Evolution has answered this need with a surprising range of tricks. Some seeds grow papery wings and spin away on the wind like tiny helicopters. Others wear hooks that snag the fur of passing animals, hitching a ride for miles. Still others hide inside sweet fruit, counting on an animal to eat them and carry them off, depositing them far away. Each method is a kind of bargain struck with the world around the plant. The seed that travels farthest is often the one with the best chance to grow where the parent's shade cannot reach.

prompt: Which choice best states the central idea of the passage?

choices.text: Standard time zones existed long before any railroads were ever built.
choices.rationale: Time zones came in response to railroads, not before them.

choices.text: Towns before railroads all happened to keep exactly the same time as one another.
choices.rationale: The passage says each town kept its own time, not the same time.

choices.text: People universally welcomed standard time the moment it was first proposed.
choices.rationale: The passage says people grumbled at first before accepting the change.

choices.text: Railroads drove the shift from local sun time to shared, standardized time zones.
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage explains how railway schedules forced a move to shared time zones.

explanation: The passage's point is that railroads pushed local time into standardized zones.

stimulus.title: When Noon Moved
stimulus.text: Before railroads, every town kept its own time, set by the sun overhead at noon. A traveler crossing the country adjusted a watch town by town, and no one minded a few minutes' difference. Railroads made the patchwork intolerable. Trains ran on schedules, and a schedule means nothing if each station defines noon differently; a missed connection could be a matter of life and death. So the railways carved the land into broad zones, each agreeing to share a single time. People grumbled at first about clocks that no longer matched the sun, but the convenience won out. The hour on the wall had become a public agreement rather than a private reading of the sky.

prompt: Which choice best states the central idea of the passage?

choices.text: Deltas are permanent landforms that never change once they have formed.
choices.rationale: The passage stresses deltas are a constantly rebuilt balance, not permanent.

choices.text: Rivers carry only clear water and never any soil from distant hills.
choices.rationale: The passage says rivers carry soil, not only water.

choices.text: A delta is built by river sediment and shrinks when that supply is cut off.
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage explains deltas as sediment-built land that erodes when the supply is trapped.

choices.text: Dams reliably help deltas grow larger by holding back excess water.
choices.rationale: Dams starve deltas by trapping sediment, not helping them grow.

explanation: The passage shows deltas as sediment-dependent land that retreats when starved of mud.

stimulus.title: Land Made of Mud
stimulus.text: A great river carries more than water; it carries soil, scoured from distant hills and ground into fine particles. Where the river finally slows and meets the sea, it can no longer hold this load, and the sediment settles out, building flat new land called a delta. Over centuries a delta can grow seaward, fanning into a maze of channels and islands. But the process depends on a steady supply of mud. When dams upstream trap that sediment, the delta is starved; waves carry off more land than the river replaces, and the coastline begins to retreat. What looks like permanent ground is, in truth, a balance constantly being rebuilt.

prompt: Which choice best states the main idea of the passage?

choices.text: The market declined because several stalls closed at around the same time.
choices.rationale: The passage notes that not a single other stall had closed.

choices.text: Mr. Owusu succeeded mainly because his vegetables cost less than his rivals' did.
choices.rationale: The passage says it was not his prices that set him apart.

choices.text: Mr. Owusu's attentiveness to customers, not his produce, made his stall special.
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage stresses that customers returned because he knew and remembered them.

choices.text: Mr. Owusu's daughter took over the stall and ran it just as he had.
choices.rationale: The daughter notices his gift; the passage does not say she took over.

explanation: The passage's point is that personal attentiveness, not produce, drew customers back.

stimulus.title: The Stall That Listened
stimulus.text: Every Saturday, Mr. Owusu set out the same vegetables on the same table, and for years his stall was just one among many. What set him apart, his daughter noticed, was not his prices but his memory. He recalled which customer's child disliked onions, who was cooking for a crowd that weekend, who had been ill and needed something gentle. He would set aside a perfect tomato for one regular and toss an extra handful of beans to another. People did not return only for the produce; they returned because, at his stall, they were known. When he finally retired, the market felt larger and lonelier, though not a single other stall had closed.

prompt: Which choice best states the central idea of the passage?

choices.text: Ice cores preserve ancient air in layered bubbles that record past atmospheres.
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage explains how layered ice traps and dates ancient air.

choices.text: Polar snow melts completely each summer before the next winter's snowfall.
choices.rationale: The passage says winter snow does not entirely melt.

choices.text: Drilling into ice destroys the trapped bubbles before they can be studied.
choices.rationale: The passage describes analyzing the bubbles, not destroying them.

choices.text: Ice cores are useful only for measuring the present-day composition of air.
choices.rationale: Cores let researchers study ancient, not just present, air.

explanation: The passage centers on ice cores as a layered record of past atmospheres.

stimulus.title: Bubbles of Old Air
stimulus.text: Each winter's snowfall on a polar ice sheet does not entirely melt; it packs down, traps tiny pockets of air, and slowly turns to ice. Layer settles on layer, year after year, so that drilling deep into the ice is a way of drilling back through time. Trapped in those bubbles is a sample of the very atmosphere that existed when the snow fell, including the gases it carried. By analyzing cores pulled from kilometers down, researchers can compare today's air with the air of tens of thousands of years ago. The ice keeps no diary of dates, yet its layers, counted carefully, let scientists assign an age to each whisper of ancient air.

prompt: Which choice best states the central idea of the passage?

choices.text: Voting has always been conducted privately throughout recorded history.
choices.rationale: The passage says voting was long public, not always private.

choices.text: Public voting by show of hands gave ordinary workers the most power.
choices.rationale: Public voting invited pressure on workers, reducing their power.

choices.text: Reformers opposed the printed ballot as an unnecessary change.
choices.rationale: Reformers pushed for the printed ballot, not against it.

choices.text: The secret ballot freed voters to choose without fear of pressure or retaliation.
choices.rationale: Correct. The passage explains how private ballots protected voters from pressure.

explanation: The passage's point is that the secret ballot protected the ordinary voter's free choice.

stimulus.title: The Secret Ballot
stimulus.text: It is easy to assume voting has always been private, but for a long time it was not. In many places people voted aloud or by a show of hands, in full view of employers, landlords, and neighbors. That openness invited pressure: a worker might think twice before voting against a boss who could see the choice. Reformers pushed for a printed ballot, marked alone and dropped in a box, so that no one need fear retaliation for an honest vote. The change seemed minor, a mere matter of paper, yet it shifted real power toward the ordinary voter, who could now decide without an audience.
